episodeOutline.title: First Bell
episodeOutline.synopsis: On her first day at Ridgemont High, Maya (the player) has ten minutes between the front doors and first bell to figure out who she's going to be here. A hallway tease, a lunchroom full of unspoken rules, and a group project gone sideways force her to choose, again and again, between fitting in, being kind, and being herself — and discover that those aren't always three different things.

characterProfiles.name: Maya
characterProfiles.note: New student navigating her first day; protagonist.

characterProfiles.name: Sam Okafor
characterProfiles.note: Quiet by default, not by nature - a sharp sense of humor he doesn't always spend, and a sketchbook full of comic panels he's slower to share. His quiet is a choice, not a limitation: he rations his humor and his art for people he decides are worth it, and has opinions on plenty of things (cafeteria pizza, vending machine candy, unconventional project ideas) that have nothing to do with how other people treat him. Given active moments across the episode - the hallway aside, the sketchbook reveal, waving new kids over, and advocating for his own idea before he's cut off - so his fate never depends solely on being rescued.

characterProfiles.name: Jordan Ellis
characterProfiles.note: Confident, magnetic, a little careless with the weight of his own jokes. Not a villain - a mirror, whose warmth toward Maya visibly shifts depending on how she responds to him. When directly confronted by consequence, his apology is incomplete by design - accountability is something he's starting to practice, not something he's finished learning. Gets one unscripted moment of real self-awareness near the end of the day, separate from anything the player does, and its weight depends on what's already happened between him, Maya, and Sam.

characterProfiles.name: Alex Torres-Mireles
characterProfiles.note: New to Ridgemont like Maya, treats the empty table as an opportunity rather than a consolation prize. Introduces their pronouns plainly, and shares - briefly, without heaviness - what it's like to do that at every new school. Their constant motion is circumstance, not identity: given one quiet, grounded moment admitting they'd like to stop being the one who always has to make the first move, so their energy reads as adaptation to instability, not a personality trait mapped onto being nonbinary.

characterProfiles.name: Devon Park
characterProfiles.note: Competitive and certain, with a specific, human reason underneath it - an older sibling's shadow at the dinner table. His certainty is a coping strategy for real pressure at home, not just competitiveness. Gets one unguarded moment where that pressure shows, and one adult (Ms. Alvarez) who names the pressure out loud without excusing the behavior it produces - so his edge reads as personal rather than a flattened stereotype.

characterProfiles.name: Ms. Alvarez
characterProfiles.note: English teacher. Assigns project groups with more intention than she lets on, and notices more than she says out loud.

scenes.title: New Kid Jitters
scenes.location: Front steps and main hallway of Ridgemont High
scenes.description: Maya stands outside the school clutching a schedule she's already memorized out of nerves. Inside, the hallway is a river of strangers who all seem to know exactly where they belong. She catches her reflection in the trophy case and has a half-second to decide how she wants to walk in.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Anticipatory anxiety - the blank page before the story starts

scenes.title: Hallway Introductions
scenes.description: Maya can't find her locker. Jordan, effortlessly confident and a little sharp-edged, notices her spinning in circles and calls out a joke that lands halfway between funny and mean, loud enough for the hallway to hear. Nearby, a quiet student named Sam flinches - like he's seen this exact game before - then mutters under his breath, just loud enough for Maya to catch: 'Cool locker technique. Very avant-garde.' It's dry, and it's funny, and it's gone as fast as it came. He didn't have to say it. He chose to.

scenes.title: The Lunchroom Crossroads
scenes.description: Maya's tray is heavy, the cafeteria is loud, and every table already seems to know who it belongs to - except the one by the window. Sam eats alone, headphones on, clearly used to it. Maya has about four seconds to decide.

variantIntros.a: Jordan spots you before you spot him, already waving like you passed some test in the hallway. 'There she is,' he calls, grinning.
variantIntros.b: Jordan notices you and waves anyway - though it's a beat slower this time, like he's still deciding what to make of the new kid who didn't just play along.
variantIntros.c: Jordan grins like you're already old friends, waving you over before you've even scanned the room.

scenes.emotionalBeat: The defining crossroads - where do I plant my flag

scenes.title: Sitting With the In-Crowd
scenes.description: The table is fun, fast, and a little cruel. Maya notices, distantly, that she's already talking a little louder than she does at home. Mid-laugh, Sam walks by with a full tray and Jordan sticks out a foot 'as a joke.' The tray goes down. The table erupts. Everyone looks at Maya to see how she'll react.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Complicity test - the cost of belonging

scenes.title: A Quiet Thank You
scenes.description: Jordan's smile tightens, but he doesn't just shrug it off this time. 'Relax, it was a joke,' he mutters - half to Maya, half to himself, like he's trying to convince someone. It doesn't quite land, and he knows it. For the rest of lunch his jokes carry a fraction less warmth aimed her way - a small, real cost. Sam catches Maya's eye and mouths 'thanks' before slipping toward the trash cans. It doesn't fix everything. It isn't nothing, either.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Quiet validation, paid for with a little of Jordan's warmth

scenes.title: The Laugh That Stuck
scenes.description: The laugh is out of Maya's mouth before she decides to let it out. Jordan's arm slides along the back of her chair like a prize just won. Sam doesn't look back on his way to the trash cans, and somehow that's worse than if he had. For the rest of the day, whenever their paths almost cross in the hallway, he finds somewhere else to look.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging bought at a price - the itch of regret

scenes.title: Changing the Subject
scenes.description: Maya jumps in with a quick change of subject - a question about the weekend, a complaint about the cafeteria pizza - anything to move the air in the room. It works, technically. The table's attention slides off the tray on the floor. Sam picks himself up without a word, and nobody quite manages to look at him while he does, Maya included. Nothing blew up. Nothing got fixed either, and the difference sits in her stomach for the rest of the period.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Smoothed over, not resolved - the quiet cost of avoiding the moment

scenes.title: Sitting With Sam
scenes.description: Sam is surprised anyone sat down, then quietly grateful, then - once it's clear Maya isn't leaving - actually funny, in the dry, unhurried way of someone who's been saving up material. He has opinions, it turns out - strong ones, about which cafeteria pizza is a crime against the concept of pizza, and why the vending machine's candy selection is 'a personal attack.' It's not shyness so much as rationing; he mentions, almost as a throwaway line, that he used to sit with a bigger group before people 'got busy.' It doesn't take a genius to hear what he's not saying. She already knows this is the table she picked, for better or worse.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Unexpected intimacy - real connection in an overlooked corner

scenes.title: His Idea, By the End
scenes.description: Sam gives a short laugh, surprised into it. 'Today, huh?' He doesn't argue - and by the time the bell rings, he's the one nodding toward a table of kids from the art room who might be worth talking to tomorrow. It started as Maya's suggestion. By the end, it's entirely his call.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Encouragement lands, but Sam owns the next step

scenes.title: The Sketchbook
scenes.description: Neither of them says much else. It isn't awkward - it's easy, in the way only two people who've stopped performing for anyone can be. Halfway through lunch, Sam slides a sketchbook across the table without a word: a half-finished comic panel, sharp and funny in a way his quiet voice never hints at. 'You draw?' Maya asks. Sam just shrugs, like it's not a big deal. It clearly is - and it isn't the first time he's shown someone. It's just the first time in a while he's decided it was worth it.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Real connection, no performance required - and a talent Sam chooses to share

scenes.title: The Door He Opened
scenes.description: Sam hesitates - then, before Maya can say anything else, he's the one waving two kids over from a nearby table, an easy 'hey, sit with us' that seems to surprise even him. Whatever door just opened, he's the one who opened it.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Sam takes the lead - agency, not just gratitude

scenes.title: A Table of Your Own
scenes.description: Another new student drifts over with the specific relief of someone who also didn't know where to sit. 'Hey, I'm Alex - they/them,' they say, already pulling out a chair before Maya can answer. 'Third new school in four years, so I've got the introduction down to a science.' They say it lightly, like a fact rather than a burden, and start building something out of nothing - two strangers deciding, on the spot, what kind of table this is going to be.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Agency in uncertainty - building instead of choosing sides

scenes.title: Building the Table Up
scenes.description: Alex lights up at the idea and is on their feet before Maya finishes the sentence, waving over two kids from the lunch line who look equally unsure where to sit. By the bell, their table of two is a table of five, and Alex looks like they just won something.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Confidence made contagious - belonging as something actively built

scenes.title: Just the Two of Them
scenes.description: Alex seems relieved Maya doesn't push for more, and the conversation settles into something unhurried - favorite shows, worst teachers, the specific dread of a locker combination. Somewhere between the second and third topic, Alex admits, quieter than their usual rhythm, that they're a little tired of always being the one who makes the first move. 'I'm good at starting over. I'd just like a place I don't have to.' It's not sadness, exactly - just honesty, sitting still for a second before the conversation moves on. By the end of lunch it doesn't feel like a consolation table at all. It just feels like a friendship starting on time.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Depth over volume - a quieter kind of belonging

scenes.title: Caught Looking
scenes.description: Alex catches Maya's eyes drifting toward Jordan's table mid-sentence and just laughs - not unkindly. 'You can want both things, you know. Doesn't make you a fraud.' It's more generous than Maya expected, and it sits with her longer than she lets on.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Honesty about wanting more than one thing - named, not judged

scenes.title: Group Project Roulette
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez reads off project groups for the semester assignment, glancing at her clipboard a beat too long, like she already knows exactly what she's doing. Whatever happened at lunch - the tray, the laugh, the deflection - Sam's name gets called right alongside Maya's, along with a sharp, competitive classmate named Devon Park, already flipping to a page he's clearly prepped in advance. Jordan catches Maya's eye across the room for half a second, like he's trying to read which version of today just happened. Sam doesn't look especially thrilled at the pairing. Given the last hour, can she blame him?
scenes.emotionalBeat: The universe insists on a second chance

scenes.title: Group Project Roulette
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez reads the groups, glancing at her clipboard a beat too long, like this pairing isn't as random as it looks. Sam's already grinning slightly when he hears his name paired with Maya's - as if the universe agrees with a decision they'd already made over lunch. Devon Park, sharp and clearly used to being the smartest person in whatever room he's in, rounds out the trio, already flipping to a page he's clearly prepped in advance. 'Try to keep up,' he says, half joking. Sam's grin doesn't move an inch.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The universe insists on a second chance

scenes.title: Group Project Roulette
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez reads the groups, and Maya's stomach dips a little when she hears Sam's name next to hers - a face she only knows as 'that quiet kid by the window.' Devon Park is already flipping to a fresh page before his own name finishes being read. Across the room, Alex catches Maya's eye and gives an exaggerated 'good luck' shrug. Whatever table they built at lunch, this is a different kind of group entirely.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The universe insists on a second chance

scenes.title: Whose Idea Wins
scenes.description: Devon opens with a pitch he's clearly rehearsed: 'This is basically what my brother did two years ago, and it got him an A. I'm not messing with a formula that works.' When Sam finally offers an idea of his own - something visual, a little unconventional, almost like the comic panels he doodles when he thinks no one's looking - Devon barely lets it land before talking over him. Sam pushes back, quietly but clearly, for the first time all day. Devon's voice sharpens: 'I can't just do something different and hope it works. I'm not doing worse than him.' The words come out faster than he means them to, and for a second the certainty cracks all the way through. Then it's gone, replaced by his usual sureness - but Maya caught it, and so, probably, did Sam. She has the floor for exactly one sentence.

variantIntros.a: Devon sizes Maya up like she's competition, not company - word travels fast about hallway comebacks.
variantIntros.b: Devon barely glances at Maya at first, more interested in staking his claim on the project than sizing her up.
variantIntros.c: Devon assumes, without asking, that Maya will go along with whatever he decides - she's given him no reason yet to think otherwise.

scenes.emotionalBeat: The real test - not the joke this time, but the follow-through

scenes.title: A Good Idea Is a Good Idea
scenes.description: Devon blinks, thrown by the pushback. He opens his mouth to argue out of habit, then stops himself. '...Fine. Yeah. Maybe I got in my head about it.' It's not an apology, but it's the first honest thing he's said all period. Sam sits up a little straighter, not just because someone spoke up for him, but because his idea is actually happening now. It was never really about being defended. It was about being heard.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Advocacy as a skill, not just a kindness

scenes.title: Splitting the Difference
scenes.description: The blended pitch takes both ideas and files off the sharpest edges. Devon looks vaguely satisfied at getting some credit; Sam looks quietly relieved his idea survived at all. Nobody's thrilled. Everybody can work with it - which, for a first day, might be its own small win.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Flexibility that still holds its ground

scenes.title: The Idea Nobody Heard
scenes.description: Devon's plan sails through unchallenged. Sam doesn't say another word for the rest of the period. Maya tells herself it wasn't a big deal. The knot in her stomach disagrees - not because Sam needed saving, but because a good idea just got buried and nobody said so.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The cost of silence isn't dramatic - it's just quiet, and it adds up

scenes.title: A Word After Class
scenes.description: As the group packs up, Ms. Alvarez catches Devon by the door - not a scolding, just a quiet aside Maya isn't quite meant to hear, but does anyway. 'Whatever's waiting for you at home, Devon, this project doesn't have to be a rerun of somebody else's. It can just be yours.' Devon doesn't have a comeback for that. He just nods, once, and heads out.
scenes.emotionalBeat: An adult naming the pressure out loud, without making it a bigger scene than it needs to be

scenes.title: End of Day
scenes.description: Backpack on, day survived, Maya has a few quiet minutes before the bus. Maya's phone buzzes - it could be Sam, it could be Jordan's group chat, it could be nothing at all yet. Tomorrow starts here.

variantIntros.a: Sam's already at the bus loop when Jordan passes by. Jordan's performance slips for half a second: 'Hey - the tray thing. That was kind of messed up on my part.' Sam doesn't quite look at him. 'Yeah,' he says. 'It was.' Jordan opens his mouth like he might say more, doesn't, and walks off. It's not resolution. It's a start, maybe.
variantIntros.b: Jordan passes by, unbothered as ever, and doesn't slow down enough to say anything at all. Sam is nowhere in sight - Maya realizes, a little late, that she doesn't actually know which bus he takes.
variantIntros.c: Jordan passes by and, for half a second, drops the performance: 'Hey - the tray thing, earlier. That was kind of messed up on my part.' Maya waits for him to say more. He doesn't. Sam is a few yards off, close enough to have heard, and says nothing at all.

scenes.emotionalBeat: Reflective calm, edged with unfinished business

scenes.title: One Real Friend
scenes.description: Maya texts Sam first. The reply comes back fast, with an exclamation point Sam clearly doesn't use often. It's not the whole school. It's one person who's glad she showed up. For a first day, that might be enough - or the start of something bigger.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Grounded belonging - small and true

scenes.title: In With the In-Crowd
scenes.description: Jordan's group chat pings with plans for tomorrow, and Maya's name is already on the list. It feels good - loud, easy, instantly visible. A text from Sam sits underneath it, unopened: 'good luck with the group project tmrw, lol.' She means to answer. She tells herself she'll answer later. Somewhere under the good feeling is the memory of a tray hitting the floor, and Jordan's half-apology that didn't quite finish the sentence.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging with an asterisk - won, but at a cost she'll have to reckon with

scenes.title: Building Something Bigger
scenes.description: Maya texts Sam an idea: what if tomorrow's table had room for more than two people - Alex, maybe even Jordan, if he's willing to lose the audience act. It's a bigger risk than picking a side. It's also the only option where nobody has to eat alone.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Earned hope - belonging as something you build, not something you're granted

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan's joke is still hanging in the air. Everyone's looking at you.
choicePoints.context: Jordan just made a public joke at Maya's expense while she struggled with her locker. Sam flinched nearby and offered a quiet, dry aside, clearly recognizing the pattern.

options.text: Fire back with a confident joke of your own
Notice Sam's flinch and gently redirect the moment - 'Rough first day for everyone, huh?'
Laugh along and play the good sport, matching the hallway's energy

choicePoints.prompt: Tray in hand, where do you sit?
choicePoints.context: Jordan is waving Maya over, with a warmth that depends on how the hallway went. Sam is alone by the window with headphones on. An empty table sits unclaimed near the glass.

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table and see what happens

choicePoints.prompt: Sam's tray is on the floor and the table is laughing. What do you do?
choicePoints.context: Jordan tripped Sam 'as a joke' in front of the whole table. Everyone is watching Maya's reaction. There's no single 'right' answer here - each response costs something different.

options.text: Help Sam up and tell Jordan that wasn't cool
Laugh along with the table
Change the subject smoothly and move the conversation on

choicePoints.prompt: Sam just admitted his old friend group 'got busy.' What do you say?
choicePoints.context: Sam has opened up a little about being left behind by his old friends. He seems unsure whether he wants comfort, distraction, or advice.

options.text: Tell him you'd both be better off making new friends - starting today
Just stay with him and let the quiet lunch be enough
Encourage him to wave a couple of other kids over right now

choicePoints.prompt: Alex is talking about starting an after-school club. What's your move?
choicePoints.context: Alex is enthusiastic and a little chaotic, treating the empty table as a blank canvas rather than a punishment.

options.text: Get excited and start inviting other kids over to build a bigger table
Keep it low-key and just enjoy getting to know Alex
Admit, mostly to yourself, that part of you is still curious about Jordan's table

choicePoints.prompt: Devon just shut down Sam's idea before it got a fair hearing. You have the floor.
choicePoints.context: The project group is deciding on a direction. Devon is loud and certain, chasing a formula that isn't really about the project. Sam has gone quiet after trying once to push back. Nobody has actually said whether Sam's idea was good or bad.

options.text: Speak up: 'Wait, I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying.'
Propose a compromise that blends both ideas
Stay quiet and let Devon's plan move forward to avoid a fight

choicePoints.prompt: Your phone is in your hand. What do you do with the last five minutes of the day?
choicePoints.context: The first day is over. Whatever happened at lunch and in the library, Maya gets to decide what tomorrow looks like.

options.text: Text Sam and ask him to sit together again tomorrow
Accept Jordan's invite to sit with the crowd tomorrow
Text Sam a bigger idea: inviting more people, even Jordan, to one table

branches.name: The Authentic Path
branches.description: Maya prioritizes real connection over visibility from the first lunch bell onward, choosing Sam's quiet table, defending his idea under pressure, and reaching out first at day's end.
branches.outcome: Maya ends the day with one genuine friendship and a clear sense of who she wants to be, even if it cost her a seat at the loudest table.

branches.name: The Fit-In Path
branches.description: Maya chases visibility and ease, sitting with Jordan's crowd, laughing along when Sam gets hurt, and accepting an invitation into the in-crowd for tomorrow, regardless of how the project conflict resolves.
branches.outcome: Maya is socially secure but privately unsettled - she got exactly what she reached for and isn't sure it was worth what she gave up, especially with an unanswered text from Sam and Jordan's half-apology still hanging in the air.

branches.name: The Bridge-Builder Path
branches.description: Maya refuses to pick a pre-existing side, building her own table with Alex, defending Sam's idea via compromise, and ending the day proposing to merge the school's separate groups.
branches.outcome: Maya ends the day as the person who makes room for others, having built something new instead of choosing between two existing options.

branches.name: The Costly Courage Path
branches.description: Maya sits with the popular table but breaks from them to defend Sam when it matters, then still drifts back toward Jordan's group the next day regardless of the project outcome.
branches.outcome: A messier, more human outcome - Maya proved she has a spine, but hasn't yet decided which group actually deserves her loyalty.

branches.name: The Smoothed-Over Path
branches.description: Maya keeps the peace at every turn - deflecting the tray incident, staying quiet when Devon steamrolls Sam's idea - prioritizing smoothness over friction at every possible cost point.
branches.outcome: Nothing ever blows up. Nothing ever quite gets resolved either. A quietly unsettling ending that rewards a second playthrough asking 'what if I'd said something?'

emotionalArc: Public vulnerability in the hallway test, softened by a glimpse of Sam's own wit
A lunchroom that already remembers how you handled the hallway - the same crossroads, subtly reweighted
Either quiet validation (at a small cost), itchy regret, or uneasy smoothing-over depending on path - each with an immediate, visible consequence

A second, higher-stakes test of the same instinct during the project conflict, framed around whether good ideas get heard, and whether Devon's certainty has a human crack in it - named out loud by an adult afterward
Reflective calm, an unscripted flicker of accountability from Jordan (its weight shaped by what came before), and a forward-looking choice about identity
Resolution: belonging framed as something built, not simply granted - with real cost attached to the easiest option

characterArcs.arc: Moves from reactive uncertainty ('who do I need to be to survive today') toward intentional identity ('who do I choose to be tomorrow'), tested three times - socially, ethically, and again at day's end - to see if the choice holds under pressure.

Shifts from resigned invisibility to cautious hope (or continued isolation) depending on whether the player notices and acts on his discomfort - but is also given his own moments of wit, creative pride, opinionated personality, and initiative (the hallway aside, the sketchbook, waving new kids over, pushing back on Devon once unprompted) that exist independent of the player's choices.

Mostly a mirror, reflecting back whatever energy the player brings - visibly warmer or cooler toward Maya depending on her hallway response - but given one unscripted moment of genuine, unresolved self-awareness at the bus loop, whose sufficiency (or insufficiency) is judged by Sam's reaction, not just stated as fact.

Represents the low-risk, high-reward option of building something new rather than choosing between existing groups. Introduces their pronouns and briefly, plainly, what it's like to do that at every new school - identity as lived experience, not just a label - and gets one grounded, still moment naming a wish for stability, distinguishing circumstance from personality.

Stays competitive and certain throughout, but is given a single unguarded moment - a sibling's shadow slipping out mid-argument - reframed as personal pressure rather than flattened stereotype, and named explicitly by an adult (Ms. Alvarez) afterward, without excusing the behavior it produced.

traitMapping.description: Rewarded when the player takes social risks with visible stakes: standing up to Jordan, sitting alone, speaking up for Sam's idea, or initiating contact rather than waiting to be included.

Rewarded when the player notices and responds to Sam's discomfort even when it costs social standing, and when they advocate for someone being overlooked. Costed, not simply penalized, when the player smooths over or joins in harm instead of naming it.

Rewarded when the player reads a room and adjusts approach without abandoning their values - deflecting conflict smoothly, proposing compromises, or building new social structures instead of picking an existing side.

relationshipDynamics.dynamic: The emotional core of the episode - a relationship that can deepen into real friendship, curdle into guilt, or never quite form, depending entirely on several separate moments of attention, with Sam given real agency at each of them.

A test of social gravity - Jordan represents the pull of easy belonging, and his visible warmth toward Maya rises and falls with how much she plays along, independent of her feelings toward Sam. His late, unscripted flicker of self-awareness keeps him human without letting him off the hook; whether it lands as enough is judged by Sam, not narrated as resolved.

A lower-stakes practice run for standing up for someone else, mirroring the earlier tray incident but in an academic setting where the player has more clear standing to intervene, and where Devon's own pressures are briefly visible and acknowledged by an adult.

An optional, lighter relationship representing the path of building something new rather than joining or rejecting existing groups, grounded in Alex's own lived experience of starting over - and their honest wish, once, to stop having to.

replayHooks: The hallway response now visibly changes Jordan's warmth in the very next scene and Devon's read of Maya in the library, so a second playthrough can test all three openings against two different crossroads.
Three genuinely different lunchroom-seating outcomes (popular table, Sam's table, empty table) mean a first playthrough only sees a third of the content.
The tray incident has three distinct, valid aftermaths (defend, join, deflect), each with its own real cost, inviting players to compare all three rather than find 'the right answer.'
The empty-table path now branches three ways of its own (recruit, bond, admit curiosity about Jordan), each coloring the rest of the day differently.

The project-conflict scene plays very differently emotionally depending on whether the player has already bonded with or hurt Sam, and Devon's one unguarded moment - plus Ms. Alvarez's follow-up - lands differently depending on what's come before.
Three distinct endings (quiet friendship, secure popularity, bridge-building) each unlock a different state flag hinting at how future episodes might treat Maya's reputation, encouraging comparison.
The 'Costly Courage' and 'Smoothed-Over' branches produce messier, more ambiguous outcomes that curious players will want to find on a second run.
Jordan's late, unscripted moment of accountability at the bus loop is followed by a variant reaction from Sam depending on the tray-incident choice - worth noticing on a replay.
educationalGoals: Model that confidence can look like standing alone, speaking up, or simply owning a decision - not just being the loudest person in the room
Show empathy as an active choice made under social cost, not just a passive feeling, with visible consequences either way
Demonstrate adaptability as flexibility in approach without loss of core values
Illustrate that first impressions are made of many small choices, not one big one, and that those choices carry forward and are remembered by other characters
Present belonging as something buildable rather than something granted by the 'right' group
Frame speaking up for someone's idea as advocacy for good ideas, not just kindness toward a person - a skill worth developing on its own terms
Show that accountability (Jordan's) and pressure (Devon's) are real and ongoing, not resolved by a single scene or a single kind act from someone else
conversationStarters: If you had to sit somewhere on your first day, which table do you think you'd actually choose - not which one you'd want to choose?
Was there a moment in the story where staying quiet felt like the safe choice? Was it?
Do you think Jordan is a bad person, or just someone who's never had to think about how his jokes land? Does his moment at the bus loop change your answer? Did it feel like enough?
Which ending felt the most 'earned' to you, and which felt like it cost the most?
Have you ever had a moment like the spilled tray - where you had half a second to decide who you were going to be?
Sam draws, jokes, and eventually leads in his own quiet way - what did you notice about him that had nothing to do with how other people treated him?
Devon has a moment where his certainty slips - what do you think is really driving him? Did Ms. Alvarez's comment change how you saw him?
Alex says they're good at starting over but would rather not have to be. Have you ever felt that way about a skill you didn't ask to develop?

designNotes: Major structural change: collapsed the three near-duplicate lunchroom setup scenes into a single scene-3-lunchroom with a 'variantIntros' field keyed to the prior hallway choice, so the graph no longer triples content for a cosmetic difference, while the hallway choice's consequence is now legible in the actual text players see (Jordan's warmth) rather than three static copies. Applied the same variantIntros pattern to scene-6-project-tension (Devon's read of Maya varies by hallway choice) and scene-7-reflection (Jordan/Sam bus-loop dynamic varies by tray-incident choice), giving Choice 1 real downstream weight without new scene IDs. Softened the tray-incident (choice-3a) trait mapping so 'join' and 'deflect' no longer read as a punished 'wrong answer' - all three now carry different, real, non-catastrophic costs, and the accompanying prose was rewritten so each option feels like a plausible, human response rather than a morality test with one correct branch. Strengthened Jordan's accountability: his line at the tray now gets pushback from him ('relax, it was a joke') rather than a clean shrug, and his bus-loop apology is now judged by Sam's actual reaction (varied by path) rather than presented as self-evidently sufficient. Added Devon's brother-line earlier in his opening pitch (not just under pressure) and added a new convergence scene, scene-6d-alvarez-checkin, where an adult explicitly names Devon's pressure as real without excusing the behavior - addressing the ethics note that his anxiety needed a counter-narrative rather than functioning purely as a flaw to route around. Expanded Sam's characterization with explicit opinions/humor unconnected to being 'the quiet one' and reframed his silence in the prose as rationed rather than anxious. Added a grounded, still moment for Alex (wanting to stop always making the first move) to decouple their energetic personality from their nonbinary identity and to avoid coding constant motion as anxiety without naming it. Added lightweight 'stateFlags' metadata to the three endings to make clear they set up different future-episode states (documented, not fully engineered, since this outline format doesn't include a runtime variable system) - flagged as an uncertainty below. Updated all choicePoint ids, branch triggeredBy paths, and defaultNextScene chains to keep the graph fully connected and reachable, with every path terminating at END through one of the three ending scenes.
uncertainties: The 'variantIntros' field is a lightweight convention for conditional text based on prior choices; it assumes the playback engine can select the right variant based on stored state. If the runtime only supports flat scene text, these should be pre-rendered into three actual scene copies at build time, or the engine needs a small templating layer - worth confirming with engineering before implementation.

The new 'stateFlags' fields on the ending scenes are descriptive placeholders for a future cross-episode state system (e.g., 'friendshipWithSam') rather than a fully specified schema - if Project MIRROR has an existing state/flag system, these should be renamed/restructured to match it rather than treated as final.

Softening the tray-incident trait mapping (removing the CONFIDENCE penalty from 'join') is a deliberate design choice to avoid a hidden 'morality meter' feel per the game designer's note - but it does mean 'joining the laugh' now nets a positive ADAPTABILITY score despite the emotional narrative treating it as a regret-laden outcome. Worth a discussion on whether trait scores and narrative tone should always point the same direction, or whether that mismatch is itself instructive.

Ms. Alvarez's check-in scene (scene-6d) adds ~30-60 seconds of playtime across all paths; total estimated playtime is kept at 15 minutes but should be play-tested to confirm it still lands in the 10-15 minute target band.

Devon's arc now relies on players noticing two separate 'brother' mentions plus Ms. Alvarez's comment to land the reframing - if playtesting shows this still reads as a flaw-to-overcome rather than an understandable pressure, consider giving Devon a fourth, non-conditional beat (e.g., a visible reaction shot in scene-6d) rather than folding it entirely into optional dialogue.
